articles.summary: Is your search strategy stuck in 2020? Peec AI [4] helps companies understand AI visibility across models, locations, and topics. Brands like ATTIO, HUGO BOSS, and N8N use it to:

articles.title: AI IS SQUEEZING MARKETING AGENCIES FROM BOTH SIDES
articles.summary: Clients expect AI-driven cost savings to reduce their retainers, while agencies that refuse to adopt AI risk falling behind competitors and in-house teams. 53% of agency owners now see AI as a significant threat, and 66% worry that junior team members will have fewer career opportunities as AI automates the foundational tasks that once served as training grounds. Larger and more specialized agencies are faring better, while generalist shops struggle most.

articles.title: BIG TECH'S STRATEGY FOR SELLING AI: DOGFOODING
articles.summary: Big Tech is using AI internally to sell it externally, making executives and employees the first users to signal value. At Meta, Nvidia, and Salesforce, leaders tie performance to AI usage while marketing presents this as proof that it works. Spending on AI is rising, yet productivity gains remain modest, with about 40% of adults using AI at work and saving only 2% of hours, and 9% of roles fully replaced. Companies adopt norms set by industry leaders, increasing demand, while massive investments in data centers, chips, and power push firms to justify usage and accelerate adoption.

articles.title: HOW HUBSPOT SOLVED THE TWO-DATE PROBLEM
articles.summary: Showing both publish and updated dates can hurt CTR if Google surfaces the older one in search results. HubSpot avoids this by displaying only the updated date in standard format while listing the original publish timing as plain text, which Google ignores. This keeps search listings looking fresh while still giving readers context, helping maintain SEO performance.

articles.title: THE BACKSTORY BEHIND CLAY'S NEW PRICING MODEL
articles.summary: Clay updated its pricing to reflect platform use beyond data, adding an “actions” metric alongside data credits and reducing tiers from 5 to 4. The team spent a year testing with agencies and customer segments to ensure fairness. Actions remain invisible for most users while guiding power users to upgrade. Transparent communication included videos, memos, and grandfathering legacy customers. Short-term revenue may drop 10%, but long-term upgrades are expected as action usage grows.

articles.title: VECTOR'S ORGANIC CONTENT STRATEGY BREAKDOWN
articles.summary: Vector drives growth with a system that turns founder insights into high-volume content. The team runs biweekly interviews and uses AI trained on each founder's voice to create LinkedIn posts. Founders stay active and post consistently. Engagement is the main lever, with 58% of follower growth coming from daily comments on relevant posts. The company page focuses on employee content. A podcast built on real internal discussions fuels the strategy, influencing 80% of open opportunities and serving as source material for posts

THE SCIENCE OF HOW AI PICKS ITS SOURCES
articles.summary: AI citations are concentrated among a few domains, with content breadth and structure outweighing rankings. About 30 domains capture 67% of citations, and top Google results are cited only 43.2% of the time. ChatGPT retrieves many sources but cites few, with 85% of pages never cited and 67% of cited URLs appearing only once. Longer, structured content covering multiple queries performs best, and placing key information in the first 30% of a page maximizes attention.

articles.title: SEARCH CONSOLE'S AVERAGE POSITION, EXPLAINED
articles.summary: Average position in GSC is an aggregated metric that often misleads and offers little actionable insight, so it is usually best ignored. An overall average position of 25 means a site ranks around page 3, since Google shows about 10 organic results per page. Query-level average position is more useful because it reflects how a specific URL ranks across users, such as averaging 1.5 if it appears in position 1 for one search and 2 for another. Special elements like image packs and AI Overviews count as positions, and appearing in them can raise a URL to position 1 even if its organic ranking is lower. Reported positions can vary due to personalization, device differences, and limited visibility of features like “People also ask,” which makes exact rankings harder to verify.

articles.title: GOODBYE TWO BY TWO. HELLO TWO BY NOTHING. A FRAMEWORK FOR BUILDING DISTINCTIVE BRANDS
articles.summary: The “two by nothing” framework shows competitors fighting over dimensions like price or quality, while the brand creates a new plane where it stands alone. Airbnb illustrates this by shifting from a place to stay to a way to experience a destination or travel together, which enables more distinctive campaigns and positioning. The approach requires mapping competitors, uncovering brand and audience insights, and reframing industry assumptions into something people care about. It works even if the product is similar, since differentiation can come from values and identity rather than features.

articles.title: THIS REDDIT WATCHDOG CHANGED EVERYTHING BRANDS THOUGHT THEY KNEW ABOUT THE PLATFORM
articles.summary: The subreddit r/companiesthatspam appeared in February to call out brands astroturfing on Reddit, quickly ranking in Google search. It was removed within a month, but the risk remains. Reddit is the top-cited domain on major AI platforms at 3.11%, ahead of YouTube and Wikipedia, so bot-driven backlash can appear in AI searches. Brands like Mint Mobile that maintain genuine community engagement outperform those using agency-run manipulation.

articles.title: WHAT HAPPENS WHEN A BRAND FAILS TO DELIVER ON ITS BASIC PROMISE
articles.summary: FedEx's slogan, "The World on Time," sets high expectations, so any delivery failure feels like a breach. Despite a 95.3% on-time rate, PwC found 1 in 3 consumers will leave a brand after one bad experience. A recent 45-hour delay on a priority shipment shows how gaps like drivers missing store hours and poor customer service cause more reputational damage than the delay itself. Brands with high expectations need recovery processes to match.
